curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III, Volume 3 (Performance Measurement), Learning Module 3: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

reading.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

drills.los_text: discuss the objectives and scope of the GIPS standards and their benefits to prospective clients and investors, as well as investment managers
drills.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

questions.stem: *Mission of the GIPS standards* *most likely* is:

options.A: to promote ethics and integrity and instill trust through the use of the GIPS standards by achieving universal demand for compliance by asset owners, adoption by asset managers, and support from regulators for the ultimate benefit of the global investment community.
options.B: The mission of the GIPS standards is to establish a mandatory global regulatory regime for performance reporting enforced by national securities regulators.
options.C: The mission is to guarantee the accuracy of every individual portfolio return a compliant firm reports to its clients.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'The mission of the GIPS standards is "to promote ethics and integrity and instill trust through the use of the GIPS standards by achieving universal demand for compliance by asset owners, adoption by asset managers, and support from regulators for the ultimate benefit of the global investment community."'
rationales.B: Incorrect. GIPS compliance is voluntary and ethics-driven — the mission is universal demand for compliance, not government enforcement.
rationales.C: Incorrect. GIPS governs composite-level presentation standards; it does not certify each portfolio-level number.

questions.stem: *Five objectives of the GIPS standards* *most likely*:

options.A: single objective.
options.B: (1) promote investor interests and instill investor confidence.
options.C: no objectives.

rationales.A: Five objectives.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) promote investor interests and instill investor confidence; (2) ensure accurate and consistent data; (3) obtain worldwide acceptance of a single standard for calculating and presenting performance; (4) promote fair, global competition among investment firms; and (5) promote industry self-regulation on a global basis.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The five objectives of the GIPS standards are to: promote investor interests and instill investor confidence; ensure accurate and consistent data; obtain worldwide acceptance of a single standard for calculating and presenting performance; promote fair, global competition among investment firms; and promote industry self-regulation on a global basis.'
rationales.C: Five objectives.

questions.stem: *Scope of the GIPS Standards for Firms* *most likely*:

options.A: Any organization involved in financial services, including custodians and consultants, may claim GIPS compliance for the services it provides.
options.B: Only firms managing more than $1 billion in discretionary assets are eligible to claim compliance.
options.C: only investment management firms and asset owners that manage assets on a discretionary basis — and compete for business — may claim compliance with GIPS Standards for Firms.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The claim is available to investment management firms and asset owners managing assets on a discretionary basis and competing for business — not service providers generally.
rationales.B: Incorrect. No asset-size threshold exists for claiming compliance.
rationales.C:
Full statement: only investment management firms and asset owners that manage assets on a discretionary basis — and compete for business — may claim compliance with GIPS Standards for Firms; asset owner that manages investments directly and/or through external managers on behalf of participants/beneficiaries/organization itself but does not compete for business would comply with GIPS Standards for Asset Owners; consultants, software houses, or third-party performance measurement providers such as custodians may not claim to be GIPS-compliant; GIPS compliance cannot be claimed for only some of a firm's products, nor for specific composites, pooled funds, or portfolios — compliance can be achieved only on a firm-wide basis.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Only investment management firms and asset owners that manage assets on a discretionary basis — and compete for business — may claim compliance with the GIPS Standards for Firms. (An asset owner that manages investments, directly and/or through the use of external managers, on behalf of participants, beneficiaries, or the organization itself — but does not compete for business — would comply with the GIPS Standards for Asset Owners.) Consultants, software houses, or third-party performance measurement providers such as custodians may not claim to be GIPS-compliant. GIPS compliance cannot be claimed for only some of an investment firm's products, nor for specific composites, pooled funds, or portfolios; compliance can be achieved only on a firm-wide basis.'

questions.stem: *Unscrupulous practices GIPS standards prevent* *most likely* include:

options.A: present returns only for best-performing portfolios.
options.B: no practices.
options.C: no manipulation.

rationales.A:
Full statement: present returns only for best-performing portfolios; base portfolio values on own unsubstantiated estimates; inflate returns by annualizing partial-period results; select most favorable measurement period (low to high point); present simulated returns as actually earned; choose particular index that selected portfolios have outperformed by greatest margin; portray growth of assets to mask difference between investment returns and client contributions; use marketing department's graphic design expertise to underplay unfavorable performance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'unscrupulous employees might attempt to gather and retain assets by misrepresenting a firm's historical record. In communicating with a prospective client or investor, they could present returns only for the best-performing portfolios as though those returns fully represented the firm's expertise in a given strategy or style; base portfolio values on their own unsubstantiated estimates of asset prices; inflate returns by annualizing partial-period results; select the most favorable measurement period, calculating returns from a low point to a high point; present simulated returns as though they had actually been earned; choose as a benchmark the particular index the selected portfolios have outperformed by the greatest margin during the preferred measurement period; portray the growth of assets in the style or strategy of interest so as to mask the difference between investment returns and client contributions; or use the marketing department's expertise in graphic design to underplay unfavorable performance data and direct the prospect's attention to the most persuasive elements of the sales presentation.'
rationales.B: Multiple abuses.
rationales.C: Multiple abuses.

questions.stem: *Prospective client vs prospective investor* distinction *most likely*:

options.A: The GIPS standards use 'prospective client' and 'prospective investor' interchangeably to mean anyone who requests marketing materials.
options.B: prospective client is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of firm's strategies and qualifies to invest in that strategy via a segregated account, irrespective of whether person/entity currently invests with firm through another strategy.
options.C: A prospective investor is any person who has expressed interest in a segregated account managed to one of the firm's strategies.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The terms are distinct — one attaches to segregated-account strategies, the other to pooled funds.
rationales.B:
Full statement: prospective client is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of firm's strategies and qualifies to invest in that strategy via a segregated account, irrespective of whether person/entity currently invests with firm through another strategy; prospective investor is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of firm's pooled funds and qualifies to invest in the pooled fund, again irrespective of any other current investments with firm.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In the context of the GIPS Standards for Firms, a prospective client is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of the firm's strategies and qualifies to invest in that strategy via a segregated account, irrespective of whether the person or entity currently invests with the firm through another strategy that the firm offers. A prospective investor is any person or entity that has expressed interest in one of the firm's pooled funds and qualifies to invest in the pooled fund, again irrespective of any other current investments with the firm.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. That describes a prospective CLIENT; a prospective INVESTOR is one interested in investing in a POOLED FUND.

questions.curriculum_ref: GIPS Objectives — prospective client vs investor

questions.stem: *Benefits of GIPS compliance to investment management firms* *most likely* include:

options.A: no benefits.
options.B: no advantage.
options.C: GIPS standards well accepted by plan sponsors so non-compliance is serious competitive impediment.

rationales.A: Multi-fold benefits.
rationales.B: Multi-fold benefits.
rationales.C:
Full statement: GIPS standards well accepted by plan sponsors so non-compliance is serious competitive impediment; RFPs routinely ask if firm is GIPS-compliant and independently verified; global recognition helps compete in international markets — compliance characterized as investment management firm's 'passport to the international marketplace'; firms may realize internal benefits — implementing GIPS standards may identify opportunities to strengthen managerial controls, improve oversight of investment operations, provide management additional comfort in accuracy of performance reporting, and improve quality of information available to firm through technological enhancements.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The practical benefits to individual firms facing the initial and ongoing expenses of GIPS compliance have increased over time. In some markets, the GIPS standards are so well accepted by plan sponsors and consultants that non-compliance is a serious competitive impediment to a firm's winning new institutional business. Requests for proposals (RFPs) in manager searches routinely ask if the responding firm is in compliance with the GIPS standards and if the firm has been independently verified. In addition, the global recognition the GIPS standards have gained helps the compliant firm to compete in international markets... Compliance with the GIPS standards has appropriately been characterized as the investment management firm's passport to the international marketplace. Because the GIPS standards reflect best practices in the calculation and presentation of investment performance, firms may also realize internal benefits.'

options.A: reading is based on 2020 edition of GIPS standards, which are effective as of 1 January 2020.
options.B: no version.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: reading is based on 2020 edition of GIPS standards, which are effective as of 1 January 2020; GIPS Reports that include performance for periods ending on or after 31 December 2020 must be prepared in accordance with the 2020 edition; 2020 edition contains three chapters: GIPS Standards for Firms, GIPS Standards for Asset Owners, and GIPS Standards for Verifiers, each with its own glossary.

Correct. Per the reading: 'This reading is based on the 2020 edition of the GIPS standards, which are effective as of 1 January 2020. GIPS Reports that include performance for periods ending on or after 31 December 2020 must be prepared in accordance with the 2020 edition of the GIPS standards.' And: 'The 2020 edition of the GIPS standards contains three chapters: the GIPS Standards for Firms, the GIPS Standards for Asset Owners, and the GIPS Standards for Verifiers, each with its own glossary.'
rationales.B: 2020 edition.
rationales.C: 2020 edition.

drills.los_text: explain the fundamentals of compliance with the GIPS standards, including the definition of the firm and the firm's definition of discretion
drills.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

options.A: no structure.
options.B: contains 39 requirements and seven recommendations.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: 39 requirements + 7 recommendations.
rationales.B:
Full statement: contains 39 requirements and seven recommendations; several core principles create the foundation for GIPS standards including properly defining the firm, providing GIPS Reports to all prospective clients and prospective pooled fund investors, adhering to applicable laws and regulations, and ensuring that information presented is not false or misleading.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Section 1 of the GIPS Standards for Firms, "Fundamentals of Compliance," contains 39 requirements and seven recommendations.' And Exhibit 1: 'Fundamentals of Compliance: Several core principles create the foundation for the GIPS standards, including properly defining the firm, providing GIPS Reports to all prospective clients and prospective pooled fund investors, adhering to applicable laws and regulations, and ensuring that information presented is not false or misleading.'
rationales.C: Section 1 structure.

options.A: no definition.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: firm must be defined as investment firm, subsidiary, or division held out to public as distinct business entity.

rationales.A: Distinct business entity.
rationales.B: Firm definition.
rationales.C:
Full statement: firm must be defined as investment firm, subsidiary, or division held out to public as distinct business entity; distinct business entity is 'unit, division, department, or office that is organizationally and functionally segregated from other units, divisions, departments, or offices and that retains discretion over the assets it manages and that should have autonomy over the investment decision-making process'; possible criteria for identifying distinct business entity: organization being a legal entity, having distinct market or client type, or using separate and distinct investment process.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The firm must be defined as an investment firm, subsidiary, or division held out to the public as a distinct business entity. The Glossary defines a distinct business entity as a "unit, division, department, or office that is organizationally and functionally segregated from other units, divisions, departments, or offices and that retains discretion over the assets it manages and that should have autonomy over the investment decision-making process." Possible criteria for identifying a distinct business entity are the organization being a legal entity, having a distinct market or client type, or using a separate and distinct investment process.'

questions.stem: *Eastern National Bank case* illustrating firm definition *most likely*:

options.A: super-regional bank whose wealth management department has two separate divisions: private client division (Eastern National Bank Wealth Management Services, offers investment management to private individuals/families) and institutional client division (Eastern Institutional Asset Advisors, serves tax-exempt non-profits including pension funds/charitable foundations, does not solicit non-institutional business).
options.B: The Eastern National Bank case illustrates that a bank must always define the GIPS firm as the entire holding company, including lending operations.
options.C: The case shows that two divisions marketing under different names must always be combined into a single GIPS firm.

rationales.A:
Full statement: super-regional bank whose wealth management department has two separate divisions: private client division (Eastern National Bank Wealth Management Services, offers investment management to private individuals/families) and institutional client division (Eastern Institutional Asset Advisors, serves tax-exempt non-profits including pension funds/charitable foundations, does not solicit non-institutional business); each division has own investment team, traders, marketing, admin, accounting; institutional division decides to achieve GIPS compliance, private client division decides not to; institutional division may become GIPS-compliant because it holds itself out as distinct business unit with own autonomous investment management, research, trading, and administrative team.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Consider the case of a super-regional bank whose wealth management department consists of two separate and distinct divisions: the private client division and the institutional client division. The private client division, called Eastern National Bank Wealth Management Services, offers investment management to private individuals and families. The institutional client division, called Eastern Institutional Asset Advisors, serves tax-exempt non-profit organizations including pension funds and charitable foundations; it does not solicit or handle non-institutional business. Each division has its own investment management team, traders, marketing department, administrative personnel, and accounting department. After a few years of operating in this manner, the institutional investment unit decides to achieve compliance with the GIPS standards, but the private client division makes a business decision not to implement the GIPS standards. The institutional client division may nonetheless be in position to become GIPS-compliant because it holds itself out to customers as a distinct business unit, with its own autonomous investment management, research, trading, and administrative team.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The firm is defined as the entity held out to clients — the wealth-management business units — not the whole bank.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Divisions held out to the market as distinct businesses may each be defined as separate GIPS firms.

options.A: Total firm assets include only discretionary, fee-paying portfolios eligible for composites.
options.B: phrase refers to aggregate fair value of all assets (whether or not discretionary or fee-paying) for which firm has investment management responsibility.
options.C: Total firm assets are measured at historical cost to avoid valuation subjectivity.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The aggregate covers ALL assets under management responsibility — discretionary or not, fee-paying or not.
rationales.B:
Full statement: phrase refers to aggregate fair value of all assets (whether or not discretionary or fee-paying) for which firm has investment management responsibility; total firm assets include assets managed by sub-advisors that firm has authority to select but do NOT include advisory-only assets or uncalled committed capital.

Correct. Per the reading: 'It also establishes the boundaries for determining total firm assets. The phrase total firm assets refers to the aggregate fair value of all assets (whether or not discretionary or fee-paying) for which a firm has investment management responsibility. Total firm assets include assets managed by sub-advisors that the firm has authority to select but do not include advisory-only assets or uncalled committed capital.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The measure is aggregate FAIR VALUE, not cost.

options.A: A firm may restate its historical composite performance to reflect a new corporate structure after a reorganization.
options.B: GIPS is silent on corporate reorganizations, leaving historical treatment to each firm's discretion.
options.C: firm that has been defined for purposes of GIPS standards may undergo subsequent changes in corporate structure or organizational design.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Changes in structure or design do not permit alteration of historical composite performance.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The standards address this directly: history is preserved through organizational change.
rationales.C:
Full statement: firm that has been defined for purposes of GIPS standards may undergo subsequent changes in corporate structure or organizational design; changes in firm's organization are NOT permitted to lead to alteration of historical performance; general rule: apart from correcting errors, historical performance is not to be altered.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A firm that has been defined for the purposes of the GIPS standards may very well undergo subsequent changes in its corporate structure or organizational design. Changes in a firm's organization are not permitted to lead to alteration of historical performance, however. Indeed, we may put it down as a general rule that, apart from correcting errors, historical performance is not to be altered.'

options.A: GIPS Standards for Firms require that all discretionary, fee-paying segregated accounts must be included in at least one composite.
options.B: Inclusion in composites is optional for segregated accounts, which firms may present individually to prospects.
options.C: Only the firm's ten largest discretionary accounts must be assigned to composites.

rationales.A:
Full statement: GIPS Standards for Firms require that all discretionary, fee-paying segregated accounts must be included in at least one composite; discretionary, fee-paying pooled funds must also be included in at least one composite if they meet a composite definition; key term is 'discretionary' although GIPS standards do not define term itself; generally, portfolio is discretionary if manager is able to implement the intended investment strategy; although both discretionary and non-discretionary portfolios are included in total firm assets, only discretionary portfolios are included in composites; if client restrictions impede investment process to such extent that strategy cannot be implemented as intended, may be presumed to render portfolio non-discretionary.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS Standards for Firms require that all discretionary, fee-paying segregated accounts must be included in at least one composite. Discretionary, fee-paying pooled funds must also be included in at least one composite if they meet a composite definition. A key term in this requirement is "discretionary," although the GIPS standards do not define the term itself. Generally speaking, a portfolio is discretionary if the manager is able to implement the intended investment strategy... Although both discretionary and non-discretionary portfolios are included in total firm assets, only discretionary portfolios are included in composites. If the client imposes restrictions on the manager's freedom to make investment decisions to buy, hold, and sell securities so as to carry out the investment strategy and achieve the portfolio's financial objectives, then the manager must consider whether the portfolio is in fact discretionary. In general, restrictions that impede the investment process to such an extent that the strategy cannot be implemented as intended may be presumed to render the portfolio non-discretionary, and it should not be included in a composite.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. ALL discretionary, fee-paying segregated accounts must be included in at least one composite — cherry-picked individual presentation is what the rule prevents.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The requirement is universal across qualifying accounts, not size-ranked.

options.A: no categories.
options.B: minimum number of years required to initially claim compliance.
options.C: no requirements.

rationales.A: Multiple categories.
rationales.B:
Full statement: minimum number of years required to initially claim compliance; documenting policies and procedures related to compliance; complying with laws and regulations; avoiding false or misleading performance information; requirements concerning distribution of GIPS Reports and lists of firm composites and pooled funds; use of total return benchmarks reflective of investment strategy; requirement to correct material errors in GIPS Report and redistribution to appropriate parties; maintenance of data and information necessary to support elements of GIPS Reports; conditions under which performance may be used or linked to that of another firm.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Other requirements under Section 1, Fundamentals of Compliance, can be broadly characterized as relating to: the minimum number of years required in order to initially claim compliance with the GIPS standards; documenting policies and procedures related to compliance; complying with laws and regulations; avoiding false or misleading performance and performance-related information; the requirements concerning the distribution of GIPS Reports and lists of firm composites and pooled funds; the use of total return benchmarks reflective of the investment strategy; the requirement to correct material errors in a GIPS Report and the redistribution of the report to the appropriate parties; the maintenance of data and information necessary to support the elements of the GIPS Reports; and conditions under which performance may be used or linked to that of another firm.'
rationales.C: Multiple requirements.

questions.stem: *GIPS standards vs local laws conflict* *most likely*:

options.A: When GIPS standards conflict with local law, the GIPS standards prevail because compliance is a voluntary higher standard.
options.B: A firm facing a conflict between GIPS and local law must abandon its compliance claim entirely.
options.C: when GIPS standards conflict with laws and/or regulations regarding calculation and presentation of performance, GIPS standards obligate firms to comply with laws and regulations AND to disclose the conflict in the GIPS Report.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Firms must FOLLOW THE LAW and disclose the conflict — local law prevails over GIPS provisions.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Compliance continues; the firm complies with law and DISCLOSES the conflict.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when GIPS standards conflict with laws and/or regulations regarding calculation and presentation of performance, GIPS standards obligate firms to comply with laws and regulations AND to disclose the conflict in the GIPS Report; firms are strongly encouraged to comply with GIPS standards in addition to applicable regulatory requirements.

Correct. Per the reading: 'When the GIPS standards conflict with laws and/or regulations regarding the calculation and presentation of performance, the GIPS standards obligate firms to comply with laws and regulations and to disclose the conflict in the GIPS Report. Firms are strongly encouraged to comply with the GIPS standards in addition to applicable regulatory requirements.'

drills.los_text: discuss requirements of the GIPS standards with respect to return calculation methodologies, including the treatment of external cash flows, cash and cash equivalents, and expenses and fees
drills.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

options.A: GIPS standards mandate use of time-weighted return (TWR).
options.B: no requirement.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: GIPS standards mandate use of time-weighted return (TWR); money-weighted returns (MWRs) may be used for portfolios meeting certain conditions; TWR is method of calculating period-by-period returns that reflects change in value and negates effects of external cash flows; except for private market investment portfolios, portfolios using TWR must be valued monthly and TWR must be calculated at least monthly as of calendar month end or last business day of month; if returns are not calculated daily and portfolio receives intra-month large cash flow, portfolio must be valued and sub-period return calculated at time of large cash flow; private market investment portfolios must be valued quarterly.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS standards mandate the use of a time-weighted return (TWR). Money-weighted returns (MWRs) may be used for portfolios meeting certain conditions (described later)... TWR is a method of calculating period-by-period returns that reflects the change in value and negates the effects of external cash flows. Except for private market investment portfolios, portfolios using TWR must be valued monthly, and the TWR must be calculated at least monthly as of the calendar month end or last business day of the month. If returns are not calculated daily and the portfolio receives an intra-month large cash flow, the portfolio must be valued and a sub-period return must be calculated at the time of the large cash flow. Private market investment portfolios (e.g., real estate and infrastructure, private equity, and similar investments that are illiquid, not publicly traded, and not traded on an exchange) must be valued quarterly.'
rationales.B: TWR mandate.
rationales.C: TWR default.

options.A: specified by GIPS.
options.B: what constitutes a large cash flow is defined by the firm.
options.C: no definition.

rationales.A: Firm-defined.
rationales.B:
Full statement: what constitutes a large cash flow is defined by the firm; usually an external cash flow of such size that it may distort return if portfolio is not valued and sub-period return is not calculated at time of cash flow; large cash flow may be defined either relative to an absolute monetary threshold or as a percentage of the portfolio or composite assets; GIPS standards do not specify quantitative definition of large external cash flows — firms must make own determinations for each composite taking into account liquidity of market segments/asset classes and nature of investment strategy.

Correct. Per the reading: 'What constitutes a large cash flow is defined by the firm. It is usually an external cash flow of such size that it may distort the return if the portfolio is not valued and a sub-period return is not calculated at the time of the cash flow. A large cash flow may be defined either relative to an absolute monetary threshold or as a percentage of the portfolio or composite assets.' Also: 'the GIPS standards do not specify a quantitative definition of large external cash flows. Taking into account the liquidity of the market segments or asset classes and the nature of the investment strategy, firms must make their own determinations for each composite.'
rationales.C: Firm-specific.

questions.stem: *Money-weighted returns permitted for certain portfolios* *most likely*:

options.A: Money-weighted returns are never permitted under the GIPS standards, which mandate time-weighted returns universally.
options.B: Firms may freely choose between MWR and TWR for any portfolio based on which presents more favorably.
options.C: firm may choose to present MWRs instead of TWRs if firm has control over external cash flows AND: (1) portfolios are closed-end, fixed life, or fixed commitment OR (2) illiquid investments are significant part of investment strategy.

rationales.A: Incorrect. MWRs are permitted where the firm controls external cash flows and the portfolio meets the closed-end/fixed-life-type criteria.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The choice is conditioned on cash-flow control and portfolio characteristics — never on favorable optics.
rationales.C:
Full statement: firm may choose to present MWRs instead of TWRs if firm has control over external cash flows AND: (1) portfolios are closed-end, fixed life, or fixed commitment OR (2) illiquid investments are significant part of investment strategy; annualized, since-inception MWRs must be calculated at least annually; for periods beginning with effective date of 2020 GIPS Standards, daily external cash flows must be used (external cash flows for periods prior to this date must be reflected on at least a quarterly basis); portfolios for which MWR is calculated must be valued at least annually and as of period end for which performance is calculated.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A firm may choose to present MWRs instead of TWRs if the firm has control over the external cash flows and: (1) the portfolios are closed-end, fixed life, or fixed commitment or (2) illiquid investments are a significant part of the investment strategy. Annualized, since-inception MWRs must be calculated at least annually. For periods beginning with the effective date of the 2020 GIPS Standards, daily external cash flows must be used. (External cash flows for periods prior to this date must be reflected on at least a quarterly basis.) Portfolios for which a money-weighted return is calculated must be valued at least annually and as of the period end for which performance is calculated.'

questions.stem: *Modified Dietz worked example* $100K on 31 May → $109K on 5 June (after $10K contribution) → $110,550 on 30 June *most likely*:

options.A: true TWR (intra-period valuation) = 0.41%: sub-period 1 return = (109,000 - 10,000 - 100,000)/100,000 = -0.01.
options.B: The Modified Dietz method and true time-weighted return always produce identical results when a mid-period contribution occurs.
options.C: The example shows the return cannot be computed because the contribution date splits the measurement period.

rationales.A:
Full statement: true TWR (intra-period valuation) = 0.41%: sub-period 1 return = (109,000 - 10,000 - 100,000)/100,000 = -0.01; sub-period 2 return = (110,550 - 109,000)/109,000 = 0.0142; TWR = (1 + -0.01)(1 + 0.0142) - 1 = 0.0041 = 0.41%; Modified Dietz weight = (30-5)/30 = 0.83; Modified Dietz = (110,550 - 100,000 - 10,000)/(100,000 + 10,000 × 25/30) = 550/108,333.33 = 0.51%; Modified IRR also gives 0.51%.

Correct. Per the reading: 'consider a portfolio with a beginning value of $100,000 as of 31 May, a value of $109,000 on 5 June (which includes a cash contribution of $10,000 received that day), and an ending value of $110,550 on 30 June... The portfolio's true time-weighted return using the intra-period valuation method is 0.41%: rt,1 = (109,000 − 10,000 − 100,000)/100,000 = −0.01; rt,2 = (110,550 − 109,000)/109,000 = 0.0142; rtwr = (1 + −0.01)(1 + 0.0142) − 1 = 0.0041 = 0.41%.' And: 'wi = (CD − Di)/CD = (30 − 5)/30 = 25/30 = 0.83. Applying the Modified Dietz formula to the same example gives a return of 0.51%: rModDietz = (110,550 − 100,000 − 10,000)/[100,000 + (10,000 × 25/30)] = 0.0051 = 0.51%.' 'Applying the Modified IRR method to the simple example used earlier in this section gives a result of 0.51%, the same as the rate of return found with the Modified Dietz method.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The methods differ precisely because Modified Dietz approximates without intra-period valuation — the example contrasts the two results.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Intra-period valuation at the cash-flow date is exactly how the true TWR handles the split.

options.A: excluded.
options.B: returns from cash and cash equivalents held in portfolios must be included in all total return calculations.
options.C: no requirement.

rationales.A: Included always.
rationales.B:
Full statement: returns from cash and cash equivalents held in portfolios must be included in all total return calculations; portfolio return will be affected by how much cash manager elects to hold, and thus return calculations must reflect contribution of cash and cash equivalents to investment results; even if management of cash balances is handled by another firm (as often in manager-of-manager arrangements), cash and cash equivalents must be included in total return calculation.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Returns from cash and cash equivalents held in portfolios must be included in all total return calculations. A primary purpose of performance measurement is to enable prospective clients and, by extension, their consultants to evaluate an investment management firm's results. Within the constraints established by a client's investment policy statement (IPS), active managers often have discretion to decide what portion of a portfolio's assets to hold in cash or cash equivalents. The portfolio return will be affected by how much cash the manager elects to hold, and thus return calculations must reflect the contribution of the cash and cash equivalents to investment results. Even if the management of cash balances is handled by another firm (as is often the case in manager-of-manager arrangements), cash and cash equivalents must be included in the total return calculation.'
rationales.C: Cash always in.

options.A: allowed.
options.B: no rule.
options.C: returns for periods of less than one year must NOT be annualized.

rationales.A: Prohibited.
rationales.B: No annualization <1 year.
rationales.C:
Full statement: returns for periods of less than one year must NOT be annualized; extrapolating partial-year returns by annualizing them would amount to a prediction about investment results for the rest of the year.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Returns for periods of less than one year must not be annualized. Extrapolating partial-year returns by annualizing them would amount to a prediction about investment results for the rest of the year.'

options.A: returns must be calculated after deduction of transaction costs incurred during period.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no distinction.

rationales.A:
Full statement: returns must be calculated after deduction of transaction costs incurred during period; transaction costs are costs of buying or selling investments (brokerage commissions, exchange fees/taxes, or spreads from internal or external brokers); custody fees should NOT be considered transaction costs even when charged per-transaction; some portfolios may pay bundled fees (any combination of investment management fees, transaction costs, custody fees, and/or administrative fees); if transaction costs cannot be identified and segregated from bundle fee, composites for institutional investors must reduce gross-of-fees return by entire amount of bundled fee or by that portion of bundled fee that includes transaction costs.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS standards require that returns be calculated after the deduction of transaction costs incurred during the period. Transaction costs are the costs of buying or selling investments. These costs typically take the form of brokerage commissions, exchange fees and/or taxes, or spreads from either internal or external brokers... Custody fees should not be considered transaction costs, even when they are charged on a per-transaction basis... Some portfolios may pay bundled fees, which can include any combination of investment management fees, transaction costs, custody fees, and/or administrative fees... If transaction costs cannot be identified (either actual transactions costs or estimated transactions costs based on a reasonable estimation method) and segregated from a bundle fee, composites for institutional investors must reduce the gross-of-fees return by the entire amount of the bundled fee or by that portion of the bundled fee that includes the transaction costs.'
rationales.B: Bundled fee treatment.
rationales.C: Bundled fee rules.

drills.los_text: explain the recommended valuation hierarchy of the GIPS standards
drills.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

options.A: no definition.
options.B: amount at which an investment could be sold in an orderly, arm's-length transaction between willing parties.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Fair value definition.
rationales.B:
Full statement: amount at which an investment could be sold in an orderly, arm's-length transaction between willing parties; valuation must be determined using objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market price for identical investment in active market on measurement date if available; fair value must include any accrued income on fixed-income securities and all other investments that earn interest income (firm may choose to recognize income on cash and cash equivalents on cash basis rather than accrual basis).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Firms are required to apply a fair value methodology when valuing assets. The GIPS standards define fair value as the amount at which an investment could be sold in an orderly, arm's-length transaction between willing parties. The valuation must be determined using the objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market price for an identical investment in an active market on the measurement date, if available. Fair value must include any accrued income on fixed-income securities and all other investments that earn interest income (the firm may choose to recognize income on cash and cash equivalents on a cash basis rather than an accrual basis).'
rationales.C: Fair value.

options.A: The top of the valuation hierarchy is the firm's internal model price, since firms know their holdings best.
options.B: Quoted prices for SIMILAR investments in active markets sit at the top of the recommended hierarchy.
options.C: objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market price for identical investment in active market on measurement date (must be used if available).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Model prices sit far down the hierarchy; objective observable market prices come first.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Similar-investment quotes are the NEXT level; identical-investment quoted prices in active markets top the hierarchy.
rationales.C:
Full statement: objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market price for identical investment in active market on measurement date (must be used if available); this is the top of the hierarchy and takes precedence over all other valuation approaches.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The valuation must be determined using the objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market price for an identical investment in an active market on the measurement date, if available.' This is Level 1 of the valuation hierarchy — objective, observable, unadjusted quoted price for identical investment in active market.

questions.stem: *Recommended valuation hierarchy alternatives when Level 1 not available* *most likely*:

options.A: in declining order of preference: (1) quoted prices for similar investments in active markets.
options.B: no alternatives.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: in declining order of preference: (1) quoted prices for similar investments in active markets; (2) quoted prices for identical or similar investments in markets that are not active (markets in which there are few transactions for the investment, prices are not current, or price quotations vary substantially over time and/or between market makers); (3) market-based inputs, other than quoted prices, that are observable for the investment; (4) subjective, unobservable inputs.

Correct. Per the reading: 'If objective, observable, unadjusted quoted market prices for identical investments in active markets on the measurement date are not available, the GIPS standards recommend the following alternatives, in declining order of preference: 1. quoted prices for similar investments in active markets. If such inputs are not available or appropriate, then investments should be valued based on: 2. quoted prices for identical or similar investments in markets that are not active (markets in which there are few transactions for the investment, the prices are not current, or price quotations vary substantially over time and/or between market makers). If such inputs are not available or appropriate, then investments should be valued based on: 3. market-based inputs, other than quoted prices, that are observable for the investment. If such inputs are not available or appropriate, then investments should be valued based on: 4. subjective, unobservable inputs.'
rationales.B: Four alternatives.
rationales.C: Descending hierarchy.

options.A: highest.
options.B: lowest in valuation hierarchy — used only when no observable inputs are available.
options.C: middle.

rationales.A: Bottom of hierarchy.
rationales.B:
Full statement: lowest in valuation hierarchy — used only when no observable inputs are available; below (in declining order): (1) objective observable unadjusted quoted market price for identical investment in active market; (2) quoted prices for similar investments in active markets; (3) quoted prices for identical/similar investments in markets that are not active; (4) market-based inputs other than quoted prices that are observable.

Correct. Per the reading, subjective, unobservable inputs are the lowest level (Level 5) in the recommended valuation hierarchy: 'If such inputs are not available or appropriate, then investments should be valued based on: 4. subjective, unobservable inputs.' This is only used when none of the higher-preference levels are available.
rationales.C: Least preferred.

questions.stem: *Composite- or pooled fund-specific valuation hierarchy* recommendation *most likely*:

options.A: A single firm-wide valuation hierarchy must be applied identically to every composite and pooled fund without exception.
options.B: Valuation hierarchies are required only for private-market composites, not liquid strategies.
options.C: GIPS standards not only require firms to document their valuation policies, procedures, methodologies, and hierarchies but also recommend that valuation hierarchies be composite- or pooled fund-specific.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The recommendation is that hierarchies be appropriate to — and may be specific to — individual composites or pooled funds.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Documentation of valuation policies applies across the firm; the composite-specific recommendation is not confined to private markets.
rationales.C:
Full statement: GIPS standards not only require firms to document their valuation policies, procedures, methodologies, and hierarchies but also recommend that valuation hierarchies be composite- or pooled fund-specific; for investment strategies employing plain-vanilla securities trading in robust markets, quoted prices are readily available; other composites may represent strategies that materially make use of securities that trade infrequently in relatively illiquid markets where values must be imputed or estimated (real estate and private equity obvious examples).

Correct. Per the reading: 'the GIPS standards not only require firms to document their valuation policies, procedures, methodologies, and hierarchies but also recommend that the valuation hierarchies be composite- or pooled fund-specific. Normally, for investment strategies that employ plain-vanilla securities trading in robust markets, quoted prices are readily available. Other composites, however, may represent strategies that materially make use of securities that trade infrequently in relatively illiquid markets where values must be imputed or estimated. Real estate and private equity are obvious examples.'

options.A: once established, valuation policies must be documented, followed consistently, and made available to prospective clients upon request.
options.B: Valuation policies are internal working documents that need not be shared with prospective clients.
options.C: Firms may vary valuation policies period to period as market conditions warrant, without documentation.

rationales.A:
Full statement: once established, valuation policies must be documented, followed consistently, and made available to prospective clients upon request; for assets valued using quantitative models, useful to list input factors such as discount rates and risk-adjusted cash flow projections and to review basis for estimating them; portfolio managers, security analysts, quantitative analysts, and traders should participate in these discussions.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Once established, the valuation policies must be documented, followed consistently, and made available to prospective clients upon request. For assets valued using quantitative models, it is useful to list input factors such as discount rates and risk-adjusted cash flow projections and to review the basis for estimating them. Portfolio managers, security analysts, quantitative analysts, and traders should participate in these discussions.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Policies must be made AVAILABLE TO PROSPECTIVE CLIENTS upon request.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Once established, policies must be documented and applied CONSISTENTLY.

drills.los_text: explain requirements of the GIPS standards with respect to composite return calculations, including methods for asset-weighting portfolio returns
drills.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

questions.stem: *Three methods for composite time-weighted return calculation* *most likely*:

options.A: single method.
options.B: (1) asset-weighting individual portfolio returns using beginning-of-period values.
options.C: no methods.

rationales.A: Three methods.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) asset-weighting individual portfolio returns using beginning-of-period values; (2) using a method that reflects both beginning-of-period values and external cash flows; (3) using the Aggregate Return method.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Time-weighted composite returns must be calculated in one of three ways: asset-weighting the individual portfolio returns using beginning-of-period values; using a method that reflects both beginning-of-period values and external cash flows; or using the Aggregate Return method.'
rationales.C: Three methods.

questions.stem: *Composite return using beginning-of-period asset weighting* *most likely* formula:

options.A: Composite returns are computed as the simple arithmetic average of the constituent portfolio returns.
options.B: Composite returns weight each portfolio by its ending-period market value.
options.C: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_0,pi / Σ V_0,pi)] where r_C is composite return, r_pi is return of individual portfolio i, V_0,pi is beginning value of portfolio i, and Σ V_0,pi is total beginning fair value of all individual portfolios in composite.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Equal weighting ignores portfolio size; the method asset-weights the constituent returns.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The formula shown weights by BEGINNING-of-period values, not ending values.
rationales.C:
Full statement: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_0,pi / Σ V_0,pi)] where r_C is composite return, r_pi is return of individual portfolio i, V_0,pi is beginning value of portfolio i, and Σ V_0,pi is total beginning fair value of all individual portfolios in composite; composite return is sum of individual portfolio returns weighted in proportion to their respective percentages of aggregate beginning assets.

Correct. Per Equation 7: 'Under the "beginning assets" weighting method, the composite return calculation is shown in Equation 7: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_0,pi / Σ_pi=1^n V_0,pi)] where rC is the composite return, rpi is the return of an individual portfolio i, V0,pi is the beginning value of portfolio i, and Σ V0,pi is the total beginning fair value of all the individual portfolios in the composite. In other words, the composite return is the sum of the individual portfolio returns weighted in proportion to their respective percentages of aggregate beginning assets.'

questions.stem: *Composite return using beginning assets plus weighted cash flows* *most likely*:

options.A: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_pi / Σ V_pi)] where V_pi = V_0 + Σ(CF_i × w_i).
options.B: no formula.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_pi / Σ V_pi)] where V_pi = V_0 + Σ(CF_i × w_i); V_pi is denominator of Modified Dietz formula (sum of beginning assets and weighted external cash flows for portfolio i); cash flows weighted by w_i = (CD - D_i)/CD, where CD is total calendar days in period and D_i is calendar days from beginning of period to time cash flow occurred.

Correct. Per Equations 6-8: 'the sum of beginning assets and weighted external cash flows, Vp, is calculated as shown in Equation 6: V_p = V_0 + Σ(CF_i × w_i)... Under the alternate "beginning assets plus weighted cash flows" method, shown in Equation 8, the return calculation uses the individual portfolios' VP, computed earlier, in place of V0,p: r_C = Σ [r_pi × (V_pi / Σ V_pi)].'
rationales.B: V_pi weighting.
rationales.C: Weighted CF method.

options.A: no data.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Four-portfolio data.
rationales.B:
Portfolio A begins $100.00K, ends $110.55K; Portfolio B begins $97.40K, ends $105.20K; Portfolio C begins $112.94K, ends $113.30K; Portfolio D begins $124.47K, ends $100.50K; total begins $434.81K, ends $429.55K; with various cash flows during June; under beginning-of-period weighting method, weight assigned to Portfolio A is 100/434.81 = 0.23 = 23%; determining composite return when there are external cash flows is more complex — cash flows weighted following methodology introduced in Modified Dietz.

Correct. Per Exhibit 5 data: 'Portfolio A begins 100.00K, ends 110.55K; Portfolio B begins 97.40K, ends 105.20K; Portfolio C begins 112.94K, ends 113.30K; Portfolio D begins 124.47K, ends 100.50K; total begins 434.81K, ends 429.55K.' And: 'Portfolio A had a beginning fair value of $100,000, and all four portfolios combined had a beginning fair value of approximately $435,000, so the weight assigned to Portfolio A is 100/434.81 = 0.23 = 23%... Determining a composite return when there are external cash flows is a little more complex. The cash flows must be weighted following the methodology introduced in our discussion of the Modified Dietz rate-of-return calculation.'
rationales.C: Real data.

options.A: annual only.
options.B: no requirement.
options.C: composite time-weighted returns, except for private market investment composites, must be calculated at least monthly.

rationales.A: Monthly minimum.
rationales.B: Monthly minimum.
rationales.C:
Full statement: composite time-weighted returns, except for private market investment composites, must be calculated at least monthly; the less frequently the asset-weighting exercise is conducted, the greater the likelihood that composite returns will inaccurately reflect the constituent portfolios' aggregate performance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Composite time-weighted returns, except for private market investment composites, must be calculated at least monthly. The less frequently the asset-weighting exercise is conducted, the greater the likelihood that composite returns will inaccurately reflect the constituent portfolios' aggregate performance.'

questions.stem: *Composite must include all portfolios meeting definition* *most likely*:

options.A: to prevent firms from presenting only their best-performing portfolios to prospective clients, GIPS standards require all actual, fee-paying, discretionary segregated accounts to be included in at least one composite.
options.B: Composites need include only portfolios whose clients have consented to performance disclosure.
options.C: Composite membership is determined annually by the firm's performance committee based on representativeness.

rationales.A:
Full statement: to prevent firms from presenting only their best-performing portfolios to prospective clients, GIPS standards require all actual, fee-paying, discretionary segregated accounts to be included in at least one composite; all actual, fee-paying, discretionary pooled funds must also be included in at least one composite if they meet a composite definition; non-discretionary segregated accounts and pooled funds must NOT be included in composites; non-fee-paying discretionary segregated accounts and pooled funds may be included but additional disclosures may be required; if a strategy is offered as a pooled fund and a segregated account, the pooled fund must be included in a composite for that strategy; firm does not need to create a composite for a strategy if offered only as a pooled fund.

Correct. Per the reading: 'To prevent firms from presenting only their best-performing portfolios to prospective clients, the GIPS standards require all actual, fee-paying, discretionary segregated accounts to be included in at least one composite. All actual, fee-paying, discretionary pooled funds must also be included in at least one composite if they meet a composite definition. Non-discretionary segregated accounts and pooled funds must not be included in composites. Non-fee-paying discretionary segregated accounts and pooled funds may be included in a composite, but additional disclosures may be required... If a strategy is offered as a pooled fund and a segregated account, the pooled fund must be included in a composite for that strategy. The firm does need not to create a composite for a strategy if it is offered only as a pooled fund.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Inclusion is mandatory for all actual, fee-paying, discretionary portfolios meeting the definition — client consent is not a filter.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Definition-based inclusion, not committee selection, prevents showcasing only the best performers.

options.A: no formula.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Weight formula.
rationales.B:
w_i = (CD - D_i)/CD where CD is total number of calendar days in period and D_i is number of calendar days since beginning of period until cash flow CF_i occurs; e.g., contribution on 5 June with 30 days in June: w_i = (30-5)/30 = 25/30 = 0.83; assumes cash flows occur at end of day; firm must consistently apply a policy for weighting of cash flows.

Correct. Per Equation 4: 'w_i = (CD − D_i)/CD where CD is the total number of calendar days in the period and Di is the number of calendar days from the beginning of the period to the time cash flow CFi occurs. (Note that this formula assumes that cash flows occur at the end of the day.) In our example, a $10,000 contribution occurs on 5 June, so Di = 5, and there are 30 days in June, so CD = 30. The proportion of the measurement period for which the $10,000 is in the portfolio is thus w_i = (CD − Di)/CD = (30 − 5)/30 = 25/30 = 0.83.'
rationales.C: Weight formula.

drills.los_text: explain the meaning of "discretionary" in the context of composite construction and, given a description of the relevant facts, determine whether a portfolio is likely to be considered discretionary
drills.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

options.A: no criterion.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: portfolio is discretionary if manager is able to implement the intended investment strategy.

rationales.A: Strategy implementation criterion.
rationales.B: Implementation criterion.
rationales.C:
Full statement: portfolio is discretionary if manager is able to implement the intended investment strategy; if constraints impede investment process to such extent that strategy cannot be implemented as intended, may be presumed to render portfolio non-discretionary; because discretion is key variable that determines inclusion in or exclusion from composite, firm implementing GIPS standards must have clear, written definition of discretion, and must consistently apply its definition.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Generally speaking, a portfolio is discretionary if the manager is able to implement the intended investment strategy... In general, restrictions that impede the investment process to such an extent that the strategy cannot be implemented as intended may be presumed to render the portfolio non-discretionary, and it should not be included in a composite... Because discretion is a key variable that determines inclusion in or exclusion from a composite, a firm implementing the GIPS standards must have a clear, written definition of discretion. The firm must consistently apply its definition of discretion.'

options.A: some clients may have environmental, social or governance (ESG) restrictions that prohibit investment in securities issued by companies operating in alcohol, tobacco, or gaming industries.
options.B: Any client-imposed ESG restriction automatically renders the portfolio non-discretionary and excludes it from composites.
options.C: ESG restrictions are ignored for discretion purposes because they reflect values rather than investment constraints.

rationales.A:
Full statement: some clients may have environmental, social or governance (ESG) restrictions that prohibit investment in securities issued by companies operating in alcohol, tobacco, or gaming industries; others might have restrictions that prohibit sale of company stock; additionally, legal restrictions may apply (e.g., public fund might be statutorily precluded from investing in non-domestic securities); NONE of these constraints automatically renders a portfolio non-discretionary; portfolio manager must determine whether client-imposed constraints are likely to materially affect ability to execute investment strategy; if not material, could include in composite with unrestricted portfolios; if material, may include in composite with similarly constrained portfolios or classify as non-discretionary.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Some clients may have environmental, social or governance (ESG) restrictions that prohibit investment in securities issued by companies operating in alcohol, tobacco, or gaming industries. Other clients might have restrictions that prohibit the sale of company stock. Additionally, legal restrictions may apply. For instance, a public fund might be statutorily precluded from investing in non-domestic securities. None of these constraints automatically renders a portfolio non-discretionary. Rather, in these and other cases, the portfolio manager must determine whether the client-imposed constraints are likely to materially affect her ability to execute the investment strategy. If the constraints are determined to not have a material effect, the manager could include the portfolio in a composite with portfolios that have no such restriction. If the constraint is material, the manager may include the portfolio in a composite with other, similarly constrained portfolios or classify it as non-discretionary and exclude the portfolio from all composites.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Restrictions are evaluated for their MATERIALITY to strategy implementation — they do not automatically remove discretion.
rationales.C: Incorrect. ESG restrictions are genuine constraints; the question is whether they materially impede the intended strategy.

questions.stem: *Client-directed legacy holdings with low cost basis* *most likely*:

options.A: Legacy low-basis holdings have no bearing on the discretion assessment because all portfolios hold legacy positions.
options.B: client could direct that legacy holdings with low cost basis must not be sold because of adverse tax consequences of realizing large gains.
options.C: A do-not-sell direction on legacy holdings always renders the entire portfolio discretionary, since the firm manages everything else freely.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Client-directed no-sell constraints can materially impede strategy implementation and thus bear directly on discretion.
rationales.B:
Full statement: client could direct that legacy holdings with low cost basis must not be sold because of adverse tax consequences of realizing large gains; retaining asset in portfolio may skew performance; if holding assets hinders manager's ability to implement intended strategy, either entire portfolio should be considered non-discretionary and excluded from firm's composites OR individual assets should be removed and remaining assets for which manager has full discretion should be included in composite; alternately, firm might include materiality threshold enabling it to consider portfolio discretionary if non-discretionary assets consist of less than certain percentage of portfolio assets.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A client could insist that the manager retain specific holdings that might or might not otherwise be held in a portfolio. For example, the client could direct that legacy holdings with a low cost basis must not be sold because of the adverse tax consequences of realizing large gains. In such cases, retaining the asset in the portfolio may skew performance, and — whether the impact is favorable or unfavorable in any given measurement period — the outcome would not reflect the results of the manager's actual discretionary investment management. If holding the assets hinders the manager's ability to implement the intended strategy, either the entire portfolio should be considered non-discretionary and excluded from the firm's composites or the individual assets should be removed and the remaining assets for which the manager has full discretion should be included in the composite. Alternately, the firm might include a materiality threshold in its policy, enabling it to consider a portfolio discretionary if the non-discretionary assets consist of less than a certain percentage of portfolio assets.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The effect depends on materiality — a large frozen position can make the portfolio non-discretionary.

options.A: always non-discretionary.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: client's investment policy might prohibit use of derivative securities such as futures, swaps, and options.

rationales.A: Strategy-dependent.
rationales.B: Depends on strategy.
rationales.C:
Full statement: client's investment policy might prohibit use of derivative securities such as futures, swaps, and options; firm must consider whether the restriction is pertinent; if portfolio manager is managing domestic mid-cap stock portfolio, fact that client prohibits use of derivatives may be irrelevant if manager's typical investment approach simply buys, holds, and sells common stocks; if use of derivative securities is central to firm's implementation of the investment mandate, however, then client's policy may render the portfolio non-discretionary.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For example, a client's investment policy might prohibit the use of derivative securities such as futures, swaps, and options. In this case, the firm must consider whether the restriction is pertinent. If a portfolio manager is managing a domestic mid-cap stock portfolio, the fact that the client prohibits the use of derivatives may be irrelevant if the manager's typical investment approach simply buys, holds, and sells common stocks. If the use of derivative securities is central to the firm's implementation of the investment mandate, however, then the client's policy may render the portfolio non-discretionary.'

options.A: in some cases, pattern of external cash flows might make a portfolio non-discretionary.
options.B: no impact.
options.C: always discretionary.

rationales.A:
Full statement: in some cases, pattern of external cash flows might make a portfolio non-discretionary; if client frequently makes large withdrawals, perhaps on regular schedule, portfolio manager might have to maintain such a high level of liquidity that he cannot truly implement the investment strategy as he does for other portfolios with similar stated investment mandate, objective, or strategy.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In some cases, the pattern of external cash flows might make a portfolio non-discretionary. For example, if a client frequently makes large withdrawals, perhaps on a regular schedule, a portfolio manager might have to maintain such a high level of liquidity that he cannot truly implement the investment strategy as he does for other portfolios with a similar stated investment mandate, objective, or strategy.'
rationales.B: Liquidity constraint.
rationales.C: Liquidity forced.

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no model or hypothetical portfolios may be included in any composite.
options.C: no distinction.

rationales.A: Model excluded + seed included.
rationales.B:
Full statement: no model or hypothetical portfolios may be included in any composite; firms may not link theoretical performance of simulated or model portfolios with actual performance (model, hypothetical, backtested, or simulated returns are all considered theoretical performance and can be shown as supplemental information but cannot be linked to actual composite or pooled fund returns); on the other hand, if firm created new strategy and managed portfolios in this strategy with its own assets (sometimes called seed money), it could include those portfolios from inception in appropriate composites, subject to any presentation and reporting requirements related to inclusion of non-fee-paying portfolios in composites.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In the process of developing, testing, and refining new investment strategies, firms frequently construct model portfolios and use historical security prices to simulate hypothetical performance in past measurement periods. No model or hypothetical portfolios may be included in any composite. Firms may not link the theoretical performance of simulated or model portfolios with actual performance. (Model, hypothetical, backtested, or simulated returns are all considered theoretical performance. These returns can be shown as supplemental information but cannot be linked to actual composite or pooled fund returns.) On the other hand, if the firm created a new strategy and managed portfolios in this strategy with its own assets — sometimes called seed money — it could include those portfolios from inception in appropriate composites.'
rationales.C: Model vs seed.

drills.los_text: explain the role of investment mandates, objectives, or strategies in the construction of composites
drills.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

options.A: no principle.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: under GIPS standards, composites must be defined according to investment mandate, objective, or strategy.

rationales.A: Mandate-based.
rationales.B: Mandate-based.
rationales.C:
Full statement: under GIPS standards, composites must be defined according to investment mandate, objective, or strategy; composites must include all portfolios that meet composite definition; composite definition, including detailed criteria that determine assignment of portfolios to composite, must be documented in firm's policies and procedures; well-defined composites will be objectively representative of firm's products and consistent with firm's marketing strategy; firms are NOT permitted to include portfolios with different investment mandates, objectives, or strategies in the same composite.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Under the GIPS standards, composites must be defined according to investment mandate, objective, or strategy; composites must include all portfolios that meet the composite definition; and the composite definition, including detailed criteria that determine the assignment of portfolios to the composite, must be documented in the firm's policies and procedures. Well-defined composites will be objectively representative of the firm's products and consistent with the firm's marketing strategy... It is a guiding principle of composite definition that firms are not permitted to include portfolios with different investment mandates, objectives, or strategies in the same composite.'

options.A: one possible hierarchy: Investment Mandate → Asset Classes → Style or Strategy → Benchmarks → Risk/Return Characteristics.
options.B: Composite definition hierarchies must begin with benchmarks, since the benchmark defines the strategy.
options.C: The GIPS standards prescribe a single mandatory hierarchy that all firms must apply without adaptation.

rationales.A:
Full statement: one possible hierarchy: Investment Mandate → Asset Classes → Style or Strategy → Benchmarks → Risk/Return Characteristics; composite based on investment mandate bears summary product/strategy description like 'Global Equities'; composite based on asset class (equity or fixed income) may be acceptable; asset-class modifier may indicate composite's investment style or strategy.

Correct. Per the reading: 'One possible hierarchy that may be helpful for the firm considering how to define composites is outlined as follows. Investment Mandate; Asset Classes; Style or Strategy; Benchmarks; Risk/Return Characteristics. A composite based on the investment mandate bears a summary product or strategy description, such as "Global Equities"... A composite based on the constituent portfolios' asset class, such as "equity" or "fixed income," may also be acceptable; however, asset classes are broadly inclusive, and because generic descriptions are not very informative, asset class composites should be offered only if they are legitimately and meaningfully representative of the firm's products.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The illustrated hierarchy runs mandate → asset class → style/strategy → benchmark → risk/return — benchmarks come later, not first.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The hierarchy is a POSSIBLE approach; firms adapt composite definitions to their own structures.

options.A: single dimension.
options.B: portfolios might be classified according to a defined style.
options.C: no matrix.

rationales.A: Cap × style.
rationales.B:
Full statement: portfolios might be classified according to a defined style; equity style matrix classifies portfolios by capitalization (large cap, mid-cap, and small cap) and by style (value; core, also called neutral, market oriented, or blend; and growth); might be starting point for defining a set of composites; fixed-income portfolios may be classified by combination of duration and/or market segment that generally aligns with major fixed-income indexes.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Portfolios might also be classified according to a defined style. An equity style matrix that classifies portfolios by capitalization (large cap, mid-cap, and small cap) and by style (value; core, also called neutral, market oriented, or blend; and growth) might be the starting point for defining a set of composites. Fixed-income portfolios may be classified by a combination of duration and/or market segment that generally aligns with the major fixed-income indexes.'
rationales.C: 3x3 matrix.

options.A: Style assignment must rely exclusively on the manager's self-declared strategy label.
options.B: Portfolios may not be assigned to style categories because styles are marketing constructs.
options.C: portfolio may be assigned to one of style categories based on money-weighted averages of pertinent characteristics of portfolio's holdings.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Assignment can be grounded in the portfolio's actual holdings characteristics (or returns-based methods), not self-labels.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Style-based composite definition is legitimate; money-weighted holdings characteristics are one recognized assignment basis.
rationales.C:
Full statement: portfolio may be assigned to one of style categories based on money-weighted averages of pertinent characteristics of portfolio's holdings; e.g., portfolio holding stocks with average market cap of $5 billion along with relatively high price-to-earnings multiple, relatively high price-to-book ratio, and relatively low dividend yield would likely be identified as mid-cap growth portfolio; alternately, portfolio's historical monthly or quarterly returns might be regressed against returns of pertinent capital market indexes to determine which style-specific benchmarks best explain portfolio's performance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A portfolio may be assigned to one of the style categories based on the money-weighted averages of pertinent characteristics of the portfolio's holdings. For example, a portfolio holding stocks with an average market capitalization of $5 billion along with a relatively high price-to-earnings multiple, a relatively high price-to-book ratio, and a relatively low dividend yield, would likely be identified as a mid-cap growth portfolio. Alternately, the portfolio's historical monthly or quarterly returns might be regressed against the returns of pertinent capital market indexes to determine which style-specific benchmarks best explain the portfolio's performance.'

options.A: useful guideline is to build set of composites that will accurately represent firm's distinct investment strategies.
options.B: unlimited composites.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: useful guideline is to build set of composites that will accurately represent firm's distinct investment strategies; with too few composites, firm risks overlooking significant differences and grouping diverse portfolios together into single, overly broad composite subject to wide dispersion of portfolio returns; with too many composites, in addition to incurring unnecessary costs, firm runs risk of creating narrowly defined groupings that are too much alike, contain too few portfolios or assets to be useful, or compromise client confidentiality.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A useful guideline is to build a set of composites that will accurately represent the firm's distinct investment strategies. With too few composites, a firm risks overlooking significant differences and grouping diverse portfolios together into a single, overly broad composite subject to a wide dispersion of portfolio returns. With too many composites, in addition to incurring unnecessary costs, the firm runs the risk of creating narrowly defined groupings that are too much alike in investment strategy, contain too few portfolios or assets to be useful, or compromise client confidentiality.'
rationales.B: Balance too few / too many.
rationales.C: Trade-off.

options.A: Composite definition should begin by surveying what competitors present, to ensure comparability.
options.B: (1) review firm's organizational structure and investment process to see if distinctive strategies can be readily identified.
options.C: The common-sense approach starts by defining as many composites as possible to maximize marketing flexibility.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The starting point is the firm's OWN organizational structure and investment process.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) review firm's organizational structure and investment process to see if distinctive strategies can be readily identified; (2) review firm's existing marketing materials to determine which strategies are offered as segregated account; also review competitors' materials and RFPs; (3) construct provisional framework using descriptive captions to identify possible composites; (4) test how well firm's fee-paying, discretionary portfolios would fit provisional framework; (5) review proposed set to ensure they satisfy GIPS requirements in aggregate; (6) document composite definitions in detail and circulate for final review.

Correct. Per the reading: '1. Review the firm's organizational structure and investment process to see if distinctive strategies can be readily identified... 2. Review the firm's existing marketing materials to determine which strategies are offered as a segregated account. If possible, review marketing materials from competitors and recently received requests for proposals (RFPs) to determine how the industry defines products similar to those the firm offers. 3. Construct a provisional framework using descriptive captions to identify possible composites. 4. Taking into consideration the clients' investment policies, test how well the firm's fee-paying, discretionary portfolios would fit the provisional framework... 5. Review the proposed set of composites to ensure that, in the aggregate, the composites are likely to satisfy the requirements of the GIPS standards. 6. Document the composite definitions in detail and circulate the definitions for final review by all affected parties within the firm.'
