rationales.C: Incorrect. Proliferating composites invites cherry-picking; the process identifies genuinely distinctive strategies.

options.A: Benchmark-based composite definitions are prohibited because benchmarks change over time.
options.B: Risk/return-based composite definitions are permitted only for hedge fund strategies.
options.C: firms may also define composites based on portfolios' benchmarks, as long as benchmarks reflect the investment strategy and firm has no other composites with same characteristics.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Benchmark-based definition is permitted when the benchmark reflects the strategy and no other composite dimension separates the portfolios.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Risk/return characteristics are a general definitional basis, not a hedge-fund carve-out.
rationales.C:
Full statement: firms may also define composites based on portfolios' benchmarks, as long as benchmarks reflect the investment strategy and firm has no other composites with same characteristics; this approach particularly appropriate if portfolios are limited to holding stocks that are held in the index; portfolios sharing distinctive risk/return profiles may reasonably be grouped together — for example, enhanced index funds with benchmark-specific targeted excess returns and tracking error tolerances might fall into natural groups; fixed-income composites can be meaningfully defined by asset classes/market segments (government debt, mortgage-backed, convertibles, high-yield), investment strategies (fundamental credit analysis, sector rotation, interest rate anticipation), or investment styles (indexing, core-plus).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Firms may also define composites based on the portfolios' benchmarks, as long as the benchmarks reflect the investment strategy and the firm has no other composites with the same characteristics. This approach is particularly appropriate if the portfolios are limited to holding stocks that are held in the index. Finally, portfolios sharing distinctive risk/return profiles may reasonably be grouped together. For example, enhanced index funds with benchmark-specific targeted excess returns and tracking error tolerances might fall into natural groups. Fixed-income composites can likewise be meaningfully defined in many dimensions. For example, composites might conform to asset classes or market segments such as government debt, mortgage-backed securities, convertible bonds, or high-yield bonds; investment strategies such as fundamental credit analysis, sector rotation, or interest rate anticipation; or investment styles such as indexing or core-plus.'

drills.los_text: explain requirements of the GIPS standards with respect to composite construction, including switching portfolios among composites, the timing of the inclusion of new portfolios in composites, and the timing of the exclusion of terminated portfolios from composites
drills.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

options.A: GIPS standards require that composites include new portfolios on a timely and consistent basis after portfolio comes under management.
options.B: New portfolios enter composites at the firm's discretion, typically when performance stabilizes.
options.C: New portfolios must wait a mandatory twelve months before composite inclusion.

rationales.A:
Full statement: GIPS standards require that composites include new portfolios on a timely and consistent basis after portfolio comes under management; firms required to establish, document, and consistently apply policy of including new portfolios in appropriate composites on timely basis; for many strategies, new portfolios should be included as of beginning of next full performance measurement period after firm receives funds; if composite returns are calculated monthly and portfolio is funded on 20 May, composite should include new portfolio as of beginning of June; may take time to invest assets particularly when portfolio funded in kind or when securities to be purchased are relatively illiquid; GIPS standards give firms some discretion — firm must establish policy on composite-by-composite basis and apply it consistently.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS standards governing composite construction require that composites include new portfolios on a timely and consistent basis after the portfolio comes under management. Firms are required to establish, document, and consistently apply a policy of including new portfolios in the appropriate composites on a timely basis. For many strategies, new portfolios should be included as of the beginning of the next full performance measurement period after the firm receives the funds. If composite returns are calculated monthly and a portfolio is funded on 20 May, the composite should include the new portfolio as of the beginning of June. It may take time to invest the assets of a new portfolio in accordance with the desired investment strategy, however, particularly when the portfolio is funded in kind... or when the securities to be purchased are relatively illiquid (e.g., in emerging markets). Accordingly, the GIPS standards give firms some discretion.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Inclusion must follow a TIMELY AND CONSISTENT policy — waiting for good performance is the abuse the rule prevents.
rationales.C: Incorrect. No twelve-month mandate exists; the firm's documented policy governs, applied consistently.

options.A: Terminated portfolios are removed from all historical composite periods to keep the composite representative of current accounts.
options.B: firm must include terminated portfolio in historical performance of appropriate composite through last full measurement period in which firm had discretion to manage portfolio to the strategy.
options.C: Terminated portfolios remain in the composite through the termination date, including the final partial period.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Removing terminated accounts from history creates survivorship bias — history must retain them.
rationales.B:
Full statement: firm must include terminated portfolio in historical performance of appropriate composite through last full measurement period in which firm had discretion to manage portfolio to the strategy; in many cases, firm loses discretion over portfolio upon being notified of pending termination; if firm calculates performance monthly and is informed on 20 May that management contract is being terminated effective 31 May and is instructed to stop trading forthwith, firm should include portfolio in composite only through 30 April; historical performance of terminated portfolios remains in the appropriate composite.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Under the GIPS standards, a firm must include a terminated portfolio in the historical performance of the appropriate composite through the last full measurement period in which the firm had the discretion to manage the portfolio to the strategy. In many cases, the firm loses its discretion over the portfolio upon being notified of a pending termination... When the firm being terminated loses its discretion over the portfolio, it should include the portfolio in the composite through the last full measurement period prior to notification of termination. To use the same example, if a firm that calculates performance monthly is informed on 20 May that its management contract is being terminated effective 31 May and is instructed to stop trading forthwith, then the firm should include the portfolio in its composite only through 30 April.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Inclusion runs through the last FULL measurement period under management, not the partial final period.

options.A: Portfolios may be moved between composites at the firm's discretion to improve composite representativeness.
options.B: Once assigned, a portfolio may never change composites for any reason.
options.C: GIPS standards stipulate that portfolios cannot be switched from one composite to another unless documented changes in portfolio's investment mandate, objective, or strategy or the redefinition of the composite make it appropriate.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Switching requires DOCUMENTED changes in the portfolio's mandate or a composite redefinition — discretionary rebalancing of membership is prohibited.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Switches are possible on documented mandate changes or composite redefinition; the bar is on undocumented moves.
rationales.C:
Full statement: GIPS standards stipulate that portfolios cannot be switched from one composite to another unless documented changes in portfolio's investment mandate, objective, or strategy or the redefinition of the composite make it appropriate; historical performance of the portfolio must remain with the original composite; this is important requirement because if firms could transfer portfolios from one composite to another at will, unethical firm might identify and exploit opportunities to improve reported performance of selected composites by re-populating them with portfolios whose investment results were most advantageous during measurement period.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS standards stipulate that portfolios cannot be switched from one composite to another unless documented changes in the portfolio's investment mandate, objective, or strategy or the redefinition of the composite make it appropriate. The historical performance of the portfolio must remain with the original composite. This is an important requirement; if the GIPS standards permitted firms to transfer portfolios from one composite to another at will, an unethical firm might identify and exploit opportunities to improve the reported performance of selected composites by re-populating them with the portfolios whose investment results were most advantageous during the measurement period.'

options.A: (1) portfolio can be switched from one composite to another if client revises mandate, objective, or strategy governing investment of portfolio assets and guideline changes are documented (e.g., client might decide to modify portfolio mandate from mid-cap value to large-cap value, or from domestic equity to global equity, while retaining same investment advisor).
options.B: Reassignment between composites is permitted whenever a portfolio's returns diverge meaningfully from the composite average, since persistent divergence indicates the portfolio no longer represents the strategy.
options.C: Reassignment of a portfolio from one composite to another requires the prior written approval of the firm's independent verifier, who must confirm that the move does not distort either composite's record.

rationales.A:
Full statement: (1) portfolio can be switched from one composite to another if client revises mandate, objective, or strategy governing investment of portfolio assets and guideline changes are documented (e.g., client might decide to modify portfolio mandate from mid-cap value to large-cap value, or from domestic equity to global equity, while retaining same investment advisor); (2) portfolio can be reassigned to another composite if the original composite is redefined in such way that portfolio no longer fits it (redefinition of existing composite should be highly unusual event; generally, if strategy changes over time, most appropriate to create new composite).

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS standards describe two conditions under which portfolios can be reassigned. First, a portfolio can be switched from one composite to another if the client revises the mandate, objective, or strategy governing the investment of portfolio assets and the guideline changes are documented... Second, a portfolio can be reassigned to another composite if the original composite is redefined in such a way that the portfolio no longer fits it. Generally, if a strategy changes over time, it is most appropriate to create a new composite; accordingly, the redefinition of an existing composite should be a highly unusual event.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Performance divergence is not a ground; a client-revised mandate or composite redefinition is required.
rationales.C: Incorrect. No verifier-approval condition exists; documentation of the qualifying change is what is required.

options.A: A significant cash flow is any external flow exceeding one percent of composite assets, as fixed by the GIPS standards.
options.B: significant cash flow is client-directed cash flow sufficiently large that it may temporarily prevent firm from implementing the strategy.
options.C: Significant cash flows are firm-initiated rebalancing transfers between portfolios in the same composite.

rationales.A: Incorrect. No fixed percentage is prescribed; the firm defines significance ex ante, composite by composite.
rationales.B:
Full statement: significant cash flow is client-directed cash flow sufficiently large that it may temporarily prevent firm from implementing the strategy; significant cash flows are more likely to be an issue for strategies such as fixed income and emerging markets, wherein liquidity of underlying securities inhibits ability to quickly invest incoming cash or to conduct orderly sale of portfolio securities to meet cash outflow; firms must define 'significant' on ex ante, composite-specific basis and must consistently follow composite-specific significant cash flow policy; in event of significant cash flows, portfolio may be temporarily removed from composite; firms may alternatively use temporary new accounts to remove effect.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In the event of significant cash flows, a portfolio may be temporarily removed from the composite. A significant cash flow is a client-directed cash flow sufficiently large that it may temporarily prevent the firm from implementing the strategy. Significant cash flows are more likely to be an issue for strategies such as fixed income and emerging markets, wherein the liquidity of the underlying securities inhibits the ability to quickly invest the incoming cash or to conduct an orderly sale of portfolio securities to meet a cash outflow. Firms must define "significant" on an ex ante, composite-specific basis and must consistently follow the composite-specific significant cash flow policy. Alternatively, firms may use temporary new accounts to remove the effect of a significant cash flow.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The concept concerns CLIENT-DIRECTED external flows large enough to disrupt strategy implementation.

options.A: no thresholds.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: firm may set minimum asset level above which portfolios are eligible for inclusion in composite.

rationales.A: Minimum threshold rules.
rationales.B: Minimum rules.
rationales.C:
Full statement: firm may set minimum asset level above which portfolios are eligible for inclusion in composite; portfolios below minimum asset level considered non-discretionary with respect to that composite; if firm establishes minimum asset level, must document policies addressing how portfolios treated if fall below minimum; firm may elect to remove portfolios month after they fall below minimum; alternatively firm may determine minimum asset level required to add portfolio is $1 million but portfolio not removed unless assets fall below $900,000; GIPS standards state any changes to composite-specific minimum asset level must NOT be applied retroactively; if portfolio removed because fell below minimum, prior performance must remain in composite.

Correct. Per the reading: 'above a certain size. Portfolios below the minimum asset level would be considered non-discretionary with respect to that composite. If a firm establishes a minimum asset level for a composite, it must document policies addressing how portfolios will be treated if they fall below the minimum. As an example, a firm may elect to remove portfolios the month after they fall below the minimum. As another example, the firm may determine that the minimum asset level required to add a portfolio to a composite is $1 million but that a portfolio will not be removed from a composite unless its assets fall below $900,000. The GIPS standards further state that any changes to a composite-specific minimum asset level must not be applied retroactively. If a portfolio is removed from a composite because it fell below the minimum, its prior performance must remain in the composite. The firm must determine if the portfolio that has been removed meets any other composite definition and include it in the appropriate composite in a timely and consistent manner.'

options.A: sample statement: 'Portfolios shall not be moved from one composite to another unless the composite is redefined or documented changes in the client's guidelines require restructuring the portfolio in such a way that another composite becomes more appropriate.
options.B: Sample switching policies authorize moves whenever the portfolio manager judges a better composite fit exists.
options.C: Policy language on composite switching is discouraged because each move is unique.

rationales.A:
Full statement: sample statement: 'Portfolios shall not be moved from one composite to another unless the composite is redefined or documented changes in the client's guidelines require restructuring the portfolio in such a way that another composite becomes more appropriate. The portfolio shall be removed from the original composite at the end of the last calendar month before the event causing the removal occurred and shall be added to the appropriate new composite at the beginning of the calendar month following the date on which the portfolio is substantially invested. The historical performance of the portfolio shall remain in the original composite.'

Correct. Per the reading: 'Here is a sample statement for a policy: "Portfolios shall not be moved from one composite to another unless the composite is redefined or documented changes in the client's guidelines require restructuring the portfolio in such a way that another composite becomes more appropriate. The portfolio shall be removed from the original composite at the end of the last calendar month before the event causing the removal occurred and shall be added to the appropriate new composite at the beginning of the calendar month following the date on which the portfolio is substantially invested. The historical performance of the portfolio shall remain in the original composite."'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Manager judgment is not a basis; the sample language conditions moves on redefinition or documented client changes.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Written policy language is exactly what the standards contemplate — the sample statement exists for that reason.

drills.los_text: explain requirements of the GIPS standards with respect to presentation and reporting
drills.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

options.A: single type.
options.B: (1) GIPS Composite Report — includes all information required by GIPS standards for a specific composite.
options.C: no types.

rationales.A: Two report types.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) GIPS Composite Report — includes all information required by GIPS standards for a specific composite; (2) GIPS Pooled Fund Report — includes all information required by GIPS standards for a specific pooled fund; firms claiming compliance must make every reasonable effort to provide GIPS Report to all prospective clients and limited distribution pooled fund investors; GIPS Report must be one that represents strategy being marketed to prospect.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Firms claiming compliance with the GIPS standards must make every reasonable effort to provide a GIPS Report to all prospective clients and limited distribution pooled fund investors. The GIPS Report must be one that represents the strategy being marketed to the prospect. There are two types of GIPS Reports: a GIPS Composite Report and a GIPS Pooled Fund Report. A GIPS Composite Report includes all of the information required by the GIPS standards for a specific composite. A GIPS Pooled Fund Report includes all of the information required by the GIPS standards for a specific pooled fund.'
rationales.C: Composite + Pooled Fund.

options.A: GIPS Composite Reports must present a minimum of ten full years of annual performance from the very first report, so newer composites cannot produce a compliant report until a decade of history exists.
options.B: A single year of annual performance is sufficient for a new composite's first GIPS Composite Report, with no obligation to extend the record in subsequent presentations.
options.C: for each GIPS Composite Report that includes time-weighted returns, GIPS standards require firms to show at least 5 years of annual performance (unless composite has been in existence for less than 5 years) and that GIPS-compliant performance record must then be extended each year until at least 10 years of performance have been presented.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The initial minimum is FIVE years (or since inception if shorter), BUILDING to ten.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Five years (or since-inception) is the floor, then annual additions up to ten.
rationales.C:
Full statement: for each GIPS Composite Report that includes time-weighted returns, GIPS standards require firms to show at least 5 years of annual performance (unless composite has been in existence for less than 5 years) and that GIPS-compliant performance record must then be extended each year until at least 10 years of performance have been presented; if composite has been in existence for less than 5 years, firm may present returns since inception and build over time to 10 years of required returns.

Correct. Per the reading: 'For each GIPS Composite Report that includes time-weighted returns, the GIPS standards require that firms show at least 5 years of annual performance (unless the composite has been in existence for less than 5 years) and that the GIPS-compliant performance record must then be extended each year until at least 10 years of performance have been presented. If the composite has been in existence for less than 5 years, the firm may present returns since inception and build over time to the 10 years of required returns.'

questions.stem: *Required elements of GIPS Composite Report (TWR)* *most likely*:

options.A: composite and benchmark annual returns for all years.
options.B: no elements.
options.C: no requirements.

rationales.A:
Full statement: composite and benchmark annual returns for all years; number of portfolios (if six or more) in composite at each period end; amount of assets in composite; amount of total firm assets at end of each period; measure of internal dispersion of individual portfolio returns for each annual period if composite contains six or more portfolios for the full year; if monthly composite returns are available, three-year annualized ex post standard deviation of composite and benchmark returns as of each annual period end.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The core elements of a GIPS Composite Report that presents a time-weighted return include the following: composite and benchmark annual returns for all years; the number of portfolios (if six or more) in the composite at each period end; the amount of assets in the composite; the amount of total firm assets at the end of each period; a measure of internal dispersion of individual portfolio returns for each annual period if the composite contains six or more portfolios for the full year; and if monthly composite returns are available, a three-year annualized ex post standard deviation of the composite and benchmark returns as of each annual period end.'
rationales.B: Six required elements.
rationales.C: Six required.

options.A: Internal dispersion disclosure is recommended but optional for composites with fewer than twenty portfolios.
options.B: GIPS standards require that for each annual period a measure of internal dispersion of returns earned by individual portfolios in composite be presented.
options.C: Internal dispersion refers to the tracking error of the composite against its benchmark.

rationales.A: Incorrect. An annual internal dispersion measure is REQUIRED (with limited exceptions for very small composites, e.g., five or fewer portfolios).
rationales.B:
Full statement: GIPS standards require that for each annual period a measure of internal dispersion of returns earned by individual portfolios in composite be presented; requirement intended to allow users to see how consistently firm implemented its strategy across individual portfolios; wide range of results should prompt recipient to inquire about possible causes of variability of returns to portfolios purportedly managed in accordance with same strategy; may suggest composite is defined too broadly; only portfolios in composite for entire year are included in calculation of this dispersion measure; GIPS standards do not limit firms to one measure of internal dispersion — method chosen should fairly represent range of returns for each annual period.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The GIPS standards require that for each annual period a measure of internal dispersion of the returns earned by individual portfolios in the composite be presented. This important requirement is intended to allow users to see how consistently the firm implemented its strategy across individual portfolios. A wide range of results should prompt the recipient of the performance presentation to inquire about possible causes of the variability of returns to portfolios that are purportedly managed in accordance with the same strategy. It may suggest, among many other possibilities, that the composite is defined too broadly to provide meaningful information... (Note that only those portfolios in the composite for the entire year are included in the calculation of this dispersion measure.)... Note that the GIPS standards do not limit firms to using one of the measures of internal dispersion described here.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Internal dispersion measures the spread of INDIVIDUAL PORTFOLIO returns within the composite, not benchmark-relative risk.

questions.stem: *Three-year annualized ex post standard deviation requirement* *most likely*:

options.A: Ex post standard deviation requirements were removed from the 2020 GIPS standards as duplicative.
options.B: The variability disclosure covers the composite only; benchmark variability is the index provider's responsibility.
options.C: 2020 GIPS Standards for Firms require that firms present information about historical variability of composite and benchmark returns.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The 2020 standards RETAIN the historical-variability presentation requirement (the three-year annualized ex post standard deviation).
rationales.B: Incorrect. Variability information covers composite AND benchmark returns.
rationales.C:
Full statement: 2020 GIPS Standards for Firms require that firms present information about historical variability of composite and benchmark returns; where monthly composite returns are available, firms must present, as of each annual period end, three-year annualized ex post standard deviation of the monthly returns of both the composite and the benchmark; rationale is to give prospective clients an indication of risk of investment strategy as executed by firms under consideration; because all GIPS Composite Reports include same risk measure and that measure is based on historical experience rather than subjective inputs, GIPS standards allow for some degree of comparability among firms claiming compliance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The 2020 GIPS Standards for Firms also require that firms present information about the historical variability of composite and benchmark returns. Specifically, the GIPS standards state that, where monthly composite returns are available, firms must present, as of each annual period end, the three-year annualized ex post standard deviation of the monthly returns of both the composite and the benchmark. The rationale is to give prospective clients an indication of the risk of an investment strategy as executed by the firms under consideration. Because all GIPS Composite Reports include the same risk measure, and that measure is based on historical experience rather than subjective inputs, the GIPS standards allow for some degree of comparability among firms that claim compliance.'

options.A: sample: 'Spinning Top Investments claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®) and has prepared and presented this report in compliance with the GIPS standards.
options.B: Compliance statements are free-form; firms draft whatever language conveys their compliance claim.
options.C: The compliance statement may appear only in documents reviewed by CFA Institute before publication.

rationales.A:
Full statement: sample: 'Spinning Top Investments claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®) and has prepared and presented this report in compliance with the GIPS standards. Spinning Top Investments has been independently verified for the periods 1 January 2011 to 31 December 2020. The verification report is available upon request. A firm that claims compliance with the GIPS standards must establish policies and procedures for complying with all the applicable requirements of the GIPS standards. Verification provides assurance on whether the firm's policies and procedures related to composite and pooled fund maintenance, as well as the calculation, presentation, and distribution of performance, have been designed in compliance with the GIPS standards and have been implemented on a firm-wide basis. Verification does not provide assurance on the accuracy of any specific performance report.'

Correct. Per Exhibit 8 Disclosure 1: 'Spinning Top Investments claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®) and has prepared and presented this report in compliance with the GIPS standards. Spinning Top Investments has been independently verified for the periods 1 January 2011 to 31 December 2020. The verification report is available upon request. A firm that claims compliance with the GIPS standards must establish policies and procedures for complying with all the applicable requirements of the GIPS standards. Verification provides assurance on whether the firm's policies and procedures related to composite and pooled fund maintenance, as well as the calculation, presentation, and distribution of performance, have been designed in compliance with the GIPS standards and have been implemented on a firm-wide basis. Verification does not provide assurance on the accuracy of any specific performance report.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Prescribed compliance language exists — the sample statement reflects the required wording for verified/unverified claims.
rationales.C: Incorrect. CFA Institute does not pre-review firm reports; the firm is responsible for correct use of the statement.

questions.stem: *Net-of-fees calculation using model management fee* *most likely*:

options.A: Net-of-fees returns must be computed using each client's actual negotiated fee, never a model fee.
options.B: per Exhibit 8 disclosure: net-of-fees returns are calculated by deducting a model management fee of 0.083%, 1/12th of the highest management fee of 1.00%, from the monthly gross composite return.
options.C: Model-fee net returns are computed using the firm's LOWEST fee schedule to show best-case results.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Model fees are permitted; the exhibit's method deducts 1/12th of the highest applicable fee monthly.
rationales.B:
Full statement: per Exhibit 8 disclosure: net-of-fees returns are calculated by deducting a model management fee of 0.083%, 1/12th of the highest management fee of 1.00%, from the monthly gross composite return; gross-of-fees returns are presented before management and custodial fees but after all trading expenses; composite and benchmark returns are presented gross of non-reclaimable withholding taxes.

Correct. Per Exhibit 8 Disclosure 7: 'Gross-of-fees returns are presented before management and custodial fees but after all trading expenses. Composite and benchmark returns are presented gross of non-reclaimable withholding taxes. Net-of-fees returns are calculated by deducting a model management fee of 0.083%, 1/12th of the highest management fee of 1.00%, from the monthly gross composite return.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Using the lowest fee would overstate net performance; the HIGHEST applicable fee is the conservative model input.

options.A: no requirement.
options.B: no template.
options.C: per Exhibit 8 Disclosure 3.

rationales.A: Description + risks + minimum + history.
rationales.B: Composite description.
rationales.C:
Full statement: per Exhibit 8 Disclosure 3: 'The Large Cap Growth Composite includes all institutional portfolios that invest in large-cap US stocks that are considered to have growth in earnings prospects that are superior to that of the average company within the XYZ Large Cap Growth Index. Key material risks include the risks that stock prices will decline and that the composite will underperform its benchmark. The account minimum for the composite is $5 million. Prior to July 2016, the account minimum was $2 million. Prior to March 2020, the name of the composite was the Growth Composite.'

Correct. Per Exhibit 8 Disclosure 3: 'The Large Cap Growth Composite includes all institutional portfolios that invest in large-cap US stocks that are considered to have growth in earnings prospects that are superior to that of the average company within the XYZ Large Cap Growth Index. Key material risks include the risks that stock prices will decline and that the composite will underperform its benchmark. The account minimum for the composite is $5 million. Prior to July 2016, the account minimum was $2 million. Prior to March 2020, the name of the composite was the Growth Composite.'

drills.los_text: explain the conditions under which the performance of a past firm or affiliation may be linked to or used to represent the historical performance of a new or acquiring firm
drills.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

questions.stem: *Four requirements for portability of past performance to acquiring firm* *most likely*:

options.A: performance from past firm or affiliation may be linked to performance of new or acquiring firm if new or acquiring firm meets certain requirements — apply on composite-specific basis: (1) substantially all investment decision makers are employed by new or acquiring firm.
options.B: Past performance always transfers with individual portfolio managers as their personal track record.
options.C: Performance portability is prohibited under the GIPS standards; every firm starts its record at inception.

rationales.A:
Full statement: performance from past firm or affiliation may be linked to performance of new or acquiring firm if new or acquiring firm meets certain requirements — apply on composite-specific basis: (1) substantially all investment decision makers are employed by new or acquiring firm; (2) decision-making process remains substantially intact and independent within new or acquiring firm; (3) new or acquiring firm has records that document and support reported performance; (4) there must be NO break in the track record between past firm or affiliation and new or acquiring firm.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The "portability" of past performance is a complex and sometimes contentious subject. Performance from a past firm or affiliation may be linked to the performance of the new or acquiring firm if the new or acquiring firm meets certain requirements. The requirements, which apply on a composite-specific basis, are that (1) substantially all the investment decision makers are employed by the new or acquiring firm, (2) the decision-making process remains substantially intact and independent within the new or acquiring firm, (3) the new or acquiring firm has records that document and support the reported performance, and (4) there must be no break in the track record between the past firm or affiliation and the new or acquiring firm.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Performance belongs to the FIRM; linking requires the portability conditions (decision-makers, process, supporting records, continuity).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Portability is permitted — conditionally — when the specified requirements are met.

options.A: A break in the track record permanently bars any use of the prior firm's performance history.
options.B: if there is break in track record between past firm and new or acquiring firm, AND if first three portability tests are met, then performance from past firm or affiliation may be USED TO REPRESENT the historical performance of new or acquiring firm.
options.C: A break in the track record may be bridged by interpolating returns across the gap.

rationales.A: Incorrect. With the other portability tests met, the prior history may still be USED (shown) — it just cannot be LINKED to the new record.
rationales.B:
Full statement: if there is break in track record between past firm and new or acquiring firm, AND if first three portability tests are met, then performance from past firm or affiliation may be USED TO REPRESENT the historical performance of new or acquiring firm — but the two performance records may NOT be linked.

Correct. Per the reading: 'If there is a break in the track record between the past firm and the new or acquiring firm, and if the first three portability tests are met, then the performance from the past firm or affiliation may be used to represent the historical performance of the new or acquiring firm — but the two performance records may not be linked.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Gaps are never filled by interpolation; the records stand unlinked on either side of the break.

options.A: Acquired non-compliant assets must be brought into compliance before the acquisition closes.
options.B: Acquired assets remain permanently exempt from GIPS compliance as legacy business.
options.C: if GIPS-compliant firm acquires another firm or affiliation, the firm is given a one-year 'grace period' to bring any non-compliant assets into compliance for future reporting periods.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The standards allow a ONE-YEAR grace period after the acquisition for bringing non-compliant assets into compliance.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The exemption is temporary — one year — not permanent.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading: 'If a GIPS-compliant firm acquires another firm or affiliation, the firm is given a one-year "grace period" to bring any non-compliant assets into compliance for future reporting periods.'

questions.stem: *Spinning Top Investments example* linking past performance *most likely*:

options.A: per Exhibit 8 disclosure 4: 'Performance prior to May 2014 occurred while the investment management team was affiliated with another firm. The investment management team has managed the composite since its inception, and the investment process has not changed. The historical performance has been linked to performance earned at Spinning Top Investments.'.
options.B: The Spinning Top example demonstrates that performance from a prior affiliation may be presented without any accompanying disclosure once the portability requirements are satisfied and the records are linked.
options.C: The example demonstrates that whenever an investment team changes firms, its composite history transfers automatically to the new employer without any conditions on process, personnel, or supporting records.

rationales.A:
Full statement: per Exhibit 8 disclosure 4: 'Performance prior to May 2014 occurred while the investment management team was affiliated with another firm. The investment management team has managed the composite since its inception, and the investment process has not changed. The historical performance has been linked to performance earned at Spinning Top Investments.'; disclosure b: 'Spinning Top investments acquired the composite through an acquisition of ABC Capital in May 2014. Firm assets prior to 2014 are not presented because the composite was not part of the firm.'

Correct. Per Exhibit 8 Disclosure 4: 'Performance prior to May 2014 occurred while the investment management team was affiliated with another firm. The investment management team has managed the composite since its inception, and the investment process has not changed. The historical performance has been linked to performance earned at Spinning Top Investments.' And footnote b: 'Spinning Top investments acquired the composite through an acquisition of ABC Capital in May 2014. Firm assets prior to 2014 are not presented because the composite was not part of the firm.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The exhibit's disclosure language exists precisely because linked prior performance must be disclosed as such.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The link rests on the portability conditions — decision-makers and process substantially intact, records available.

options.A: firm-wide only.
options.B: portability requirements apply on composite-specific basis (not firm-wide).
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Composite-specific.
rationales.B:
Full statement: portability requirements apply on composite-specific basis (not firm-wide); performance can be ported for specific composites where all requirements are met, but is not automatically applied firm-wide; each composite must be evaluated for portability independently based on whether substantially all investment decision makers are employed by new firm, decision-making process remains substantially intact and independent, new firm has records documenting and supporting reported performance, and no break in track record.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The requirements, which apply on a composite-specific basis, are that (1) substantially all the investment decision makers are employed by the new or acquiring firm, (2) the decision-making process remains substantially intact and independent within the new or acquiring firm, (3) the new or acquiring firm has records that document and support the reported performance, and (4) there must be no break in the track record between the past firm or affiliation and the new or acquiring firm.'
rationales.C: Composite-specific.

options.A: no requirement.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: decision-making process must remain substantially intact AND independent within new or acquiring firm.

rationales.A: Substantially intact + independent.
rationales.B: Intact + independent.
rationales.C:
Full statement: decision-making process must remain substantially intact AND independent within new or acquiring firm; both continuity (substantially intact) and independence within the new firm are required — if the process is subsumed into a different process at the new firm or ceases to operate independently, portability may fail; combined with requirement that substantially all investment decision makers are employed by new firm — key personnel must remain and continue to make decisions in essentially the same way.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The requirements, which apply on a composite-specific basis, are that (1) substantially all the investment decision makers are employed by the new or acquiring firm, (2) the decision-making process remains substantially intact and independent within the new or acquiring firm.' Both 'substantially intact' and 'independent' modifiers apply to the decision-making process at the acquiring firm.

drills.los_text: discuss the purpose, scope, and process of verification
drills.reading_name: Overview of the Global Investment Performance Standards

options.A: verification may be characterized as process in which independent expert assesses firm's policies and procedures for constructing composites and calculating and presenting performance in light of requirements of GIPS standards.
options.B: Verification is a mandatory annual audit required to maintain any claim of GIPS compliance.
options.C: Verification certifies the accuracy of each composite's reported returns.

rationales.A:
Full statement: verification may be characterized as process in which independent expert assesses firm's policies and procedures for constructing composites and calculating and presenting performance in light of requirements of GIPS standards; verification is intended to provide firm and users of its GIPS Reports greater confidence in its claim of compliance with GIPS standards; verification does NOT provide assurance as to accuracy of any particular composite or pooled fund presentation; GIPS standards RECOMMEND that firms undergo verification (voluntary).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Verification may be informally and unofficially characterized as a process in which an independent expert assesses a firm's policies and procedures for constructing composites and calculating and presenting performance in light of the requirements of the GIPS standards. Verification is intended to provide the firm and the users of its GIPS Reports greater confidence in its claim of compliance with the GIPS standards. Verification does not provide assurance as to the accuracy of any particular composite or pooled fund presentation... The GIPS standards recommend that firms undergo verification.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Verification is VOLUNTARY (though recommended); compliance can be claimed without it, with required disclosure.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Verification assesses firm-wide POLICIES AND PROCEDURES for composite construction and GIPS-consistent presentation — not each return's accuracy.

options.A: single composite.
options.B: verification must be performed by qualified and independent third party.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Firm-wide only.
rationales.B:
Full statement: verification must be performed by qualified and independent third party; provides assurance on whether firm's policies and procedures related to composite and pooled fund maintenance, as well as calculation, presentation, and distribution of performance have been designed in compliance with GIPS standards and have been implemented on firm-wide basis; verifier must conduct verification in accordance with GIPS Standards for Verifiers; single verification report is issued only with respect to whole firm — verification CANNOT be carried out on a single composite or pooled fund; if firm does not meet all requirements of GIPS standards, it may not state or represent that it is in compliance — firm cannot claim single composite is 'in compliance' or all equity strategies are 'in compliance.'

Correct. Per the reading: 'Verification, which must be performed by a qualified and independent third party, provides assurance on whether the firm's policies and procedures related to composite and pooled fund maintenance, as well as the calculation, presentation, and distribution of performance have been designed in compliance with the GIPS standards and have been implemented on a firm-wide basis. A verifier must conduct the verification in accordance with the GIPS Standards for Verifiers. A single verification report is issued only with respect to the whole firm; verification cannot be carried out on a single composite or pooled fund. If a firm does not meet all the requirements of the GIPS standards, it may not state or in any other way represent that it is in compliance with the GIPS standards — a firm cannot claim that a single composite is "in compliance," or that all the equity strategies are "in compliance."'
rationales.C: Firm-wide.

options.A: A performance examination is simply the formal name given to the standard verification engagement once the verifier's report has been issued to the firm.
options.B: Performance examinations are mandatory annual engagements for a compliant firm's five largest composites, measured by composite assets at each year-end.
options.C: firm that is verified may choose to have detailed performance examination conducted on one or more specific composites or pooled funds, and it may state that a composite has been examined if a performance examination report has been issued for the specific composite.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Examination is a distinct, DETAILED engagement on specific composites or pooled funds, available only in addition to verification.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Examinations are optional and firm-selected, not size-mandated.
rationales.C:
Full statement: firm that is verified may choose to have detailed performance examination conducted on one or more specific composites or pooled funds, and it may state that a composite has been examined if a performance examination report has been issued for the specific composite; performance examination is separate from verification (which is firm-wide) and applies to specific composite(s) or pooled fund(s).

Correct. Per the reading: 'A firm that is verified may choose to have a detailed performance examination conducted on one or more specific composites or pooled funds, and it may state that a composite has been examined if a performance examination report has been issued for the specific composite.'

options.A: verifiers must learn about the firm, including its corporate structure and how it operates, and they must understand the firm's policies and procedures for complying with all applicable requirements and adopted recommendations of the GIPS standards.
options.B: Verifiers confine their work to recalculating a sample of composite returns.
options.C: Verifiers rely on management's representations without independent understanding of firm operations.

rationales.A:
Full statement: verifiers must learn about the firm, including its corporate structure and how it operates, and they must understand the firm's policies and procedures for complying with all applicable requirements and adopted recommendations of the GIPS standards; verifiers must not only obtain copy of firm's GIPS-related policies and procedures but also ensure that all applicable policies and procedures are properly included and adequately documented; finally, verifiers must understand policies, procedures, and methodologies the firm uses to value portfolios and compute investment performance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Verifiers must learn about the firm, including its corporate structure and how it operates, and they must understand the firm's policies and procedures for complying with all applicable requirements and adopted recommendations of the GIPS standards. Verifiers must not only obtain a copy of the firm's GIPS-related policies and procedures but also ensure that all applicable policies and procedures are properly included and adequately documented. Finally, verifiers must understand the policies, procedures, and methodologies the firm uses to value portfolios and compute investment performance.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Verifiers must UNDERSTAND the firm — structure, operations, policies, procedures, and methodologies — beyond recalculation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Independent understanding and testing, not bare reliance on representations, define the engagement.

options.A: Verification requires testing every portfolio and composite at the firm without exception.
options.B: although verification is conducted on firm-wide basis, verifiers may use sampling methodology to conduct required testing.
options.C: Sampling is prohibited in verification because it leaves untested accounts.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Verification is firm-wide in SCOPE, but verifiers may use sampling to conduct the required testing.
rationales.B:
Full statement: although verification is conducted on firm-wide basis, verifiers may use sampling methodology to conduct required testing; when selecting sample portfolios, verifiers must take into consideration number of composites and pooled funds at firm and number of portfolios in each composite; in addition, verifiers must take into account total assets under management, internal control structure at firm, number of years being verified, systems used in construction and maintenance of composites, method of calculating performance, and whether firm uses external performance measurement services; if verifier encounters errors or discovers that firm's record-keeping is deficient, larger sample or additional verification procedures may be warranted.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Although verification is conducted on a firm-wide basis, verifiers may use a sampling methodology to conduct the required testing. When selecting sample portfolios, verifiers must take into consideration the number of composites and pooled funds at the firm and the number of portfolios in each composite. In addition, verifiers must also take into account the total assets under management, the internal control structure at the firm, the number of years being verified, the systems used in the construction and maintenance of composites, the method of calculating performance, and whether the firm uses external performance measurement services. The selection of sample accounts for testing is a critical step in the verification process. If the verifier encounters errors or discovers that the firm's record-keeping is deficient, a larger sample or additional verification procedures may be warranted.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Sampling methodologies are expressly permitted.

options.A: Verification provides no substantive benefit to the firm beyond supplying marketing language for pitch books and requests for proposals submitted to institutional prospects.
options.B: The only benefit of undergoing verification is the satisfaction of securities-regulatory requirements that apply to firms presenting performance to prospective clients.
options.C: in addition to making claim of compliance on firm-wide basis more credible, verification process may benefit firm in other ways: increased knowledge in performance measurement team, consistently higher quality of performance presentations, improved internal processes and procedures, and potential marketing advantages.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Benefits include improved internal knowledge of the standards, stronger policies and procedures, and credibility of the compliance claim.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Verification is not a regulatory requirement; its benefits are credibility and process improvement.
rationales.C:
Full statement: in addition to making claim of compliance on firm-wide basis more credible, verification process may benefit firm in other ways: increased knowledge in performance measurement team, consistently higher quality of performance presentations, improved internal processes and procedures, and potential marketing advantages; above all, verification supports guiding principles of fair representation and full disclosure of investment performance.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In addition to making the claim of compliance on a firm-wide basis more credible, however, the verification process may benefit the firm in other ways: increased knowledge in the performance measurement team, consistently higher quality of performance presentations, improved internal processes and procedures, and potential marketing advantages. Above all, verification supports the guiding principles of fair representation and full disclosure of investment performance.'
